name: Berlin Airlift 1948-1949
description: A history notebook on the Berlin Airlift showing how air corridors, runway capacity, and urban demand turned a political confrontation into a logistics contest.

sides.name: Western Allies
sides.description: The U.S. and Britain built a logistics machine whose credibility depended on repetitive, disciplined air throughput rather than dramatic battlefield actions.

subUnits.name: U.S. Airlift Task Force
subUnits.description:
American C-47 and C-54 operations formed the backbone of early and later high-volume airlift into Berlin.
- Role: Heavy-lift air bridge
- Geographic anchor: Centered on Tempelhof and corridor traffic into the U.S. sector of the city.
- Why it matters: The U.S. contribution turned the blockade into a throughput competition rather than a surrender timetable.
- What to watch: Aircraft availability, load discipline, weather, and turnaround tempo.

subUnits.name: Royal Air Force Berlin Airlift
subUnits.description:
British airlift activity concentrated on sustaining its sectors and sharing the burden of the corridor system.
- Role: Sector sustainment and corridor traffic
- Geographic anchor: Operated strongly through RAF Gatow and the British sector, while sharing the wider corridor rhythm of the lift.
- Why it matters: The airlift only worked because multiple national systems accepted common discipline and timing.
- What to watch: Weather resilience, interoperability, and night or winter operations.

subUnits.name: West Berlin municipal administration
subUnits.description:
City authorities were the demand manager of the crisis, translating aircraft arrivals into electricity, heat, and food for residents.
- Role: Urban demand and distribution management
- Geographic anchor: Centered on the city sectors and the urban network receiving supplies from the airfields.
- Why it matters: The airlift's success depended on whether supplies reached citizens quickly enough to prevent political collapse.
- What to watch: Coal distribution, civilian morale, and winter vulnerability.

sides.name: Soviet blockade authorities
sides.description: The Soviet Union attempted to pressure the Western sectors by cutting land and water access while avoiding direct air conflict inside the established corridors.

subUnits.name: Soviet blockade authorities
subUnits.description:
The blockade combined political pressure with physical denial of road, rail, and barge access into West Berlin.
- Role: Access denial and coercive pressure
- Geographic anchor: Controlled the land and water approaches around the city while leaving the formal air corridors open.
- Why it matters: The blockade defined the crisis by forcing the Western response into the air.
- What to watch: Duration of pressure and whether logistical exhaustion could produce political concessions.

events.title: Currency reform sharpens the crisis
events.description: Western moves on currency reform intensified Soviet concerns over the political future of Germany and accelerated the standoff that soon reached Berlin's transport arteries.

events.title: The Soviet blockade begins
events.description: Road, rail, and barge access to the Western sectors was interrupted, making the air corridors the only workable route for sustaining West Berlin.

events.title: The sustained airlift begins
events.description: The Western allies committed to continuous supply flights, transforming a temporary workaround into the central strategy for resisting the blockade.

events.title: Tegel expands the airfield network
events.description: The rapid development of Tegel helped reduce congestion and gave the airlift more margin as winter demand and traffic intensity rose.

events.title: Easter Parade demonstrates peak throughput
events.description: Record tonnage on Easter Parade day proved that the airlift had matured into a disciplined, high-output logistics system capable of defeating the blockade politically.

events.title: The blockade is lifted
events.description: The Soviet Union reopened land access, but flights continued for months to build reserves and prove that the city would not again be easily coerced.

meta.name: Tempelhof Airport
meta.description: Tempelhof became the most iconic visual symbol of the airlift and one of its most important receiving airfields.

meta.name: RAF Gatow
meta.description: RAF Gatow was one of the main receiving fields and an anchor for the British share of the airlift.

meta.name: Tegel Airport
meta.description: Tegel provided extra capacity and helped the airlift absorb the scale and rhythm demanded by the blockade.

meta.name: Western air corridors into Berlin
meta.description: Simplified representation of the corridor structure that allowed continuous access from western Germany into Berlin.

meta.name: West Berlin demand basin
meta.description: A reminder that the airlift was serving a living city whose demand profile set the bar for success.
